pathophysiology_summary: Tinea corporis ('ringworm') is a superficial dermatophyte infection of glabrous skin, producing annular, erythematous, pruritic plaques with an active raised, scaly leading edge and central clearing that enlarge centrifugally; it is often acquired from animals (here a new kitten — zoonotic Microsporum) or humans/fomites and is contagious. It is benign and responds to topical antifungals. The ground-truth task is pattern recognition and differentiation from pityriasis rosea (Christmas-tree pattern, collarette scale, no active fungal edge), nummular eczema (no active scaly advancing border) and granuloma annulare (smooth, non-scaly), and to recognise when oral antifungal therapy is needed (scalp involvement/tinea capitis, a kerion, extensive disease, immunosuppression, or failure of topical therapy) and to AVOID topical corticosteroids (which cause 'tinea incognito'). Contagion/zoonosis control and treating the animal source are important.

differential.evidence_basis: Annular plaques with an active scaly edge and central clearing, enlarging, after new-kitten contact.
Christmas-tree pattern/collarette scale without an active fungal edge — argues against.
Coin-shaped patches but no active advancing scaly border — argues against.
Scalp involvement or a boggy inflammatory mass needs oral antifungal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Tinea corporis leads; scalp/kerion/extensive disease (needing oral antifungal) must be screened and topical steroids avoided.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic annular active-edge lesions, no scalp/kerion, limited extent, no prior steroid — topical-treatable tinea corporis.
No scalp involvement/kerion; escalation to oral antifungal if it develops or disease is extensive.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative scalp/kerion/extent screen, topical-treatable tinea corporis leads; topical antifungal + contagion/zoonosis measures, avoiding topical steroids.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; clears with 2+ weeks of topical antifungal; oral therapy if extensive/scalp/refractory.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is topical-treatable tinea corporis.

red_flags.description: Features needing oral antifungal / different management — scalp involvement (tinea capitis), a boggy painful inflammatory mass (kerion), extensive disease, immunosuppression, or prior topical-steroid use (tinea incognito)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These need oral antifungals and/or specialist input; topical steroids worsen and mask tinea.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treated/masked tinea, scarring alopecia from untreated kerion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Steroids cause tinea incognito — an atypical, worsening, poorly-scaling infection; avoid steroids.
Scalp involvement needs ORAL antifungal (topicals don't penetrate hair follicles).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical antifungal to lesions + margin, contagion/zoonosis measures (treat the kitten via a vet); oral antifungal / review if scalp/kerion/extensive/refractory.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing tinea capitis/kerion; masking tinea with steroids.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Tinea corporis: topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine or an azole) to the lesion and a margin of normal skin, continued for 1–2 weeks after clearing. Use oral antifungals for tinea capitis, kerion, extensive or refractory disease. Avoid topical corticosteroids; address animal/human sources and fomites.
